pages.raw_text:
SEATTLE, July 12.-President
A. B. Stickney of the Chicago Great
Western. who is in Seattle attend
ing the fair, believes that justice is
on the side of the inland cities in
their fights for more equitable
rates. Stickney is one of the most
Independent railroad magnates in
the country and was on the side of
Roosevelt in his encounters with
the transportation companies.


President Roosevelt's enforce
ment of the anti-rebate law has
done something to eliminate the
secret rate evil" said President
Stickney, though 1t fails to pre-
vent the discrimination as to rates
between cities. The decision in the
Spokane rate case aims at the
latter evil 1t is apparently the
purpose not to permit railroads to
build up one community and one
set of merchants at the expense of
another.


IF, for example, it is determined
that the rate from st. Paul to Sc
attic, which is lower than the rate
from st. Paul to Spokane, yields a
fair compensation to the railroad,
then it seems to be decided that
the rate to Spokane must be cor
respondingly reduced. The element
of water competition does not en-
ter into the issue largely


Not until all the railroads are
consolidated or the government
proceeds to do directly what it now
attempts to do by indirect means;
that is, fixes absolutely all railroad
rates, will there be an era of sta
bility, peace and fair play for both
the railroads and the public" con-
tinued Mr.. Stickney. Competition
between the railroads makes low
rates at competitive points, but
they are obliged to recoup by ex-
cessive charges at non-competitive
points. This is most unjust and
people have right to complain"
